subject: maths
title: Arithmetic Progressions

nodes.title: Solve arithmetic progression problems
nodes.brief: Use the AP definition, the nth-term formula, and the sum formula together to answer term, count, and sum questions about real-world and pure-number lists.
nodes.quote: In this chapter, we shall discuss one of these patterns in which succeeding terms are obtained by adding a fixed number to the preceding terms. We shall also see how to find their nth terms and the sum of n consecutive terms, and use this knowledge in solving some daily life problems.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognise/model as an AP and extract a and d.
Given a situation or list, first confirm it is an AP (constant difference between consecutive terms) and identify the first term a and common difference d. For word problems, the fixed step is d and the starting value is a.

PART 2 — Choose the right tool: nth-term vs sum.
For a single term at position n, use a_n = a + (n-1)d. For the total of the first n terms, use S_n = n/2[2a + (n-1)d] (or n/2(a+l) when first and last terms are known). Picking the sum formula when only one term is asked (or vice versa) is the main error.
keyMoves: Identify whether the list is an AP and find a and d
Choose nth-term vs sum formula based on what is asked
Substitute and solve for the unknown quantity
misconceptions: Applying the sum formula when only a single term is asked (or vice versa)
Forgetting to first verify the list is actually an AP

nodes.title: Define an AP via its common difference
nodes.brief: Recognise that an AP is a list where each term after the first is the previous term plus a fixed number d, the common difference.
nodes.quote: So, an arithmetic progression is a list of numbers in which each term is obtained by adding a fixed number to the preceding term except the first term.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — An AP has a constant gap d between consecutive terms.
A list is an AP exactly when each term after the first equals the previous term plus a fixed number d (the common difference): a_{k+1} = a_k + d. The gap between any two consecutive terms is the same throughout.

PART 2 — d can be positive, negative, or zero.
d > 0 gives an increasing AP (2, 5, 8, ...), d < 0 a decreasing one (10, 7, 4, ...), and d = 0 a constant list (3, 3, 3, ...). Crucially, AP means ADDING a fixed number — not multiplying (that would be a GP).
keyMoves: State that succeeding term = preceding term + d
Recognise d may be positive, negative, or zero
misconceptions: Believing d must be positive
Confusing 'adding a fixed number' (AP) with 'multiplying by a fixed number' (GP)

nodes.title: Compute d by subtracting consecutive terms
nodes.brief: Find the common difference by subtracting any term from the term that immediately follows it: d = a_{k+1} − a_k.
nodes.quote: d = aₖ₊₁ – aₖ
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — d = (later term) - (earlier term), always next minus previous.
To find the common difference, subtract any term from the term immediately after it: d = a_{k+1} - a_k. The order matters: it is ALWAYS next-minus-previous, never previous-minus-next.

PART 2 — Keep the sign even when the result is negative.
If the next term is smaller, d is negative — that is correct, not an error. Example: for 3/2, 1/2, ..., d = 1/2 - 3/2 = -1. Subtracting larger-from-smaller regardless of position would flip the sign and give the wrong d.
keyMoves: Pick two consecutive terms
Subtract the earlier from the later: later − earlier
Keep the sign even when the result is negative
misconceptions: Subtracting the larger from the smaller regardless of order
Computing previous − next, flipping the sign of d

nodes.title: Test whether a list is an AP
nodes.brief: Check that a₂−a₁, a₃−a₂, a₄−a₃, … are all equal; if any differs, the list is not an AP.
nodes.quote: As a₂ – a₁ ≠ a₃ – a₂, the given list of numbers does not form an AP.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute several consecutive differences, not just one.
To test if a list is an AP, compute a2-a1, a3-a2, a4-a3, and so on (at least three differences). If they are ALL equal, the list is an AP with that common difference.

PART 2 — One unequal difference means it is NOT an AP.
If even a single difference differs from the others, the list is not an AP. Checking only the first difference is risky — e.g. 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2 has a first difference of 0 but is not an AP. Always verify several differences match.
keyMoves: Compute at least the first three consecutive differences
Compare them for equality
Conclude AP (with that d) or not-AP
misconceptions: Checking only the first difference and assuming the rest match
Mistaking a list of repeated patterns (e.g. 1,1,1,2,2,2) for an AP

nodes.title: Write the next terms of an AP
nodes.brief: Once d is known, generate subsequent terms by repeatedly adding d to the last term.
nodes.quote: The next two terms are: 22 + 6 = 28 and 28 + 6 = 34.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Add d to the last known term to get the next term.
Once you know d, extend the AP by adding d to the most recent term: next = last + d. Then the one after is that result + d again, and so on.

PART 2 — Continue from the last term, with the correct sign of d.
Always add to the LAST term, not restart from the first. Watch the sign of d: if d is negative, the terms decrease. Example: AP 4, 10, 16, 22 with d=6; next terms are 22+6=28 and 28+6=34.
keyMoves: Take the last known term
Add d to get the next term
Repeat to extend the list
misconceptions: Adding the wrong-signed d
Restarting from the first term instead of continuing from the last

nodes.title: List an AP given a and d
nodes.brief: Given the first term a and common difference d, write out the AP a, a+d, a+2d, a+3d, ….
nodes.quote:
if the first term a is 6 and the common difference d is 3, then the AP is
6, 9, 12, 15, . . .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Build the list as a, a+d, a+2d, a+3d, ...
Given the first term a and common difference d, write the AP by repeatedly adding d: a, a+d, a+2d, a+3d, and so on. Example: a=6, d=3 gives 6, 9, 12, 15, ...

PART 2 — You need BOTH a and d.
Knowing a alone or d alone does not determine the AP — both are required. The kth term is a + (k-1)d. Add d successively to the correct (most recent) term, not to a each time.
keyMoves: Write a as the first term
Add d successively to produce a+d, a+2d, …
misconceptions: Thinking a alone or d alone determines the AP
Adding d to the wrong term when generating

nodes.title: Identify the first term a of an AP
nodes.brief: Read off a as the first number in the list.
nodes.quote: Since a is the first term, it can easily be written.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — a is the first entry of the list.
The first term a is simply the first number in the AP. Example: in 3/2, 1/2, -1/2, ..., a = 3/2. This basic value feeds the d-computation and both the nth-term and sum formulas.

PART 2 — Mind reversed-order problems.
Do not mistake the second term for a. In 'kth term from the last' problems where you reverse the AP, the new first term a becomes the original LAST term — so a can change depending on the direction you read the list.
keyMoves: Locate the first number in the given list
Record it as a
misconceptions: Taking the second term as a
When given 'reverse order' problems, forgetting a changes to the last term

nodes.title: Apply the nth-term formula aₙ = a + (n−1)d
nodes.brief: Compute any term of an AP by substituting a, n, and d into aₙ = a + (n−1)d.
nodes.quote: So, the nth term aₙ of the AP with first term a and common difference d is given by aₙ = a + (n – 1) d.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — a_n = a + (n-1)d, with multiplier (n-1).
To find the term at position n, substitute a, d, and n into a_n = a + (n-1)d. The multiplier on d is (n-1), NOT n — because the first term already uses zero d's. Example: a=2, d=5, n=10 gives a_10 = 2 + 9(5) = 47.

PART 2 — Substitute carefully, keeping a and d distinct.
Identify which value is a (first term) and which is d (common difference) before substituting. A frequent error is using n instead of (n-1), which overshoots by one d.
keyMoves: Identify a, d, and n
Substitute into a + (n−1)d
Simplify to the term value
misconceptions: Using n instead of (n−1) as the multiplier
Mixing up a and d in substitution

nodes.title: Find which term equals a given value
nodes.brief: Set aₙ equal to the target value and solve a + (n−1)d = value for n.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Set a_n equal to the target value and solve for n.
To find WHICH term equals a given value X, substitute X for a_n in a + (n-1)d = X and solve the linear equation for n. Example: -81 = 21 + (n-1)(-3) gives -102 = (n-1)(-3), so n-1 = 34, n = 35.

PART 2 — Solve for n, not for a_n, and watch sign of d.
The unknown is the position n, so isolate n. When d is negative, be careful with signs while transposing. The answer n is a position number (e.g. 'the 35th term').
keyMoves: Set a + (n−1)d equal to the target
Solve the linear equation for n
Report the position n
misconceptions: Solving for aₙ instead of n
Sign errors when d is negative

nodes.title: Check term membership using integer-n test
nodes.brief: A number is a term of the AP only if solving a + (n−1)d = value yields a positive integer n.
nodes.quote: But n should be a positive integer (Why?). So, 301 is not a term of the given list of numbers.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Solve a + (n-1)d = value, then test whether n is a positive integer.
To decide if a number is a term of the AP, solve for n as usual. The number IS a term only if n comes out as a positive whole number.

PART 2 — A fractional or non-positive n means NOT a member.
Example: 301 = 5 + (n-1)6 gives n = 151/3, which is not an integer — so 301 is NOT a term. Concluding membership from any solved n (even a fraction) is the error; n must be a positive integer.
keyMoves: Solve for n as in find-which-term
Check whether n is a positive integer
Conclude member (integer) or non-member (fraction)
misconceptions: Concluding membership from any solved n, even a fraction
Ignoring the positivity requirement on n

nodes.title: Find a and d from two given terms
nodes.brief: Write each known term as a + (k−1)d, then solve the pair of simultaneous equations for a and d.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write each known term as a + (k-1)d.
When two terms are given, express each as an equation: the kth term is a + (k-1)d. Example: a3 = 5 gives a + 2d = 5; a7 = 9 gives a + 6d = 9. Use (k-1), not k.

PART 2 — Subtract to eliminate a, find d, then back-substitute.
Subtract the two equations to cancel a: (a+6d) - (a+2d) = 9 - 5 gives 4d = 4, d = 1. Then a = 5 - 2(1) = 3. So a = 3, d = 1. The main errors are writing a + kd instead of a + (k-1)d, and arithmetic slips when subtracting.
keyMoves: Express each known term via a + (k−1)d
Subtract the equations to eliminate a and find d
Back-substitute to find a
misconceptions: Writing aₖ = a + kd instead of a + (k−1)d
Arithmetic slip when subtracting the two equations

nodes.title: Count the number of terms in a finite AP
nodes.brief: Treat the last term as aₙ = l, solve l = a + (n−1)d for n to get the term count.
nodes.quote:
i.e., n = 29 + 1 = 30
So, there are 30 two-digit numbers divisible by 3.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Treat the last term as a_n = l and solve l = a + (n-1)d for n.
To count the terms of a finite AP, set the last term l equal to a + (n-1)d and solve for n. Example: for 12, 15, ..., 99 with d=3, 99 = 12 + (n-1)(3) gives 87 = 3(n-1), n-1 = 29, n = 30.

PART 2 — Remember the +1 (off-by-one).
After isolating n-1, ADD 1 to get n. Forgetting this +1 undercounts by one. The count n is the number of terms (here 30), not n-1 (which is 29).
keyMoves: Identify a, d, and the last term l
Solve l = a + (n−1)d for n
Report n as the number of terms
misconceptions: Forgetting the +1 (off-by-one) when isolating n
Using a wrong last term

nodes.title: Find the kth term from the last term
nodes.brief: Either count total terms then convert to a position from the start, or reverse the AP (new a = l, new d = −d) and take the kth term.
nodes.quote: If we write the given AP in the reverse order, then a = – 62 and d = 3 (Why?)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Method A: convert to a position from the start.
First count the total terms n. The kth term from the last is the (n - k + 1)th term from the start. Example: 10, 7, 4, ..., -62 has 25 terms, so the 11th from the last is the (25 - 11 + 1) = 15th from the start = -32.

PART 2 — Method B: reverse the AP.
Reverse the list so the new first term a = l (the original last term) and the new common difference is -d (negated). Then the kth term from the original last is just the kth term of the reversed AP. Example: reversed a = -62, d = +3, so a_11 = -62 + 10(3) = -32. Watch the off-by-one and the sign of d when reversing.
keyMoves: Either count n then compute position (n − k + 1) from the start
Or reverse the AP: a becomes l, d becomes −d, then apply nth-term
misconceptions: Using the 14th term instead of the 15th (off-by-one from the last)
Forgetting to negate d when reversing

nodes.title: Model a real-world situation as an AP
nodes.brief: Translate a uniformly-changing quantity (salary, interest, plant rows, production) into an AP and extract a and d.
nodes.quote: It is an AP as the difference between the consecutive terms in the list is 80, i.e., d = 80. Also, a = 80.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — A fixed change per step signals an AP; extract a and d.
When a quantity changes by the SAME amount each step, it forms an AP. Identify the starting value as a and the repeated fixed change as d. Example: simple interest growing by Rs 80 each year is an AP with d = 80, a = 80.

PART 2 — Map the question onto a_n or S_n, and beware of GP traps.
Once a and d are set, use a_n for a single term and S_n for a total. Caution: only ADDITIVE (fixed-amount) changes are APs — compound interest or doubling is MULTIPLICATIVE (a GP), not an AP. Label the first term carefully.
keyMoves: Identify the repeating fixed change as d
Identify the starting value as a
Map the question onto aₙ or Sₙ
misconceptions: Treating compound-interest / multiplicative growth as an AP
Mislabelling the first term

nodes.title: Derive a and d when aₙ is given as a formula
nodes.brief: When the nth term is given as an expression in n, generate the first few terms to read off a and confirm constant d.
nodes.quote:
List of numbers becomes 5, 7, 9, 11, . . .
Here, 7 – 5 = 9 – 7 = 11 – 9 = 2 and so on.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Substitute n = 1, 2, 3 into the given formula to list terms.
When the nth term is given as a formula in n, generate the first few terms by substituting n = 1, 2, 3. Example: a_n = 3 + 2n gives a_1 = 5, a_2 = 7, a_3 = 9.

PART 2 — Read a from a_1 and confirm constant d.
The first term a is a_1 (compute it; do NOT just read the constant term). Confirm the differences are equal to get d. For a LINEAR formula a_n = pn + q, the common difference d is the coefficient of n (here d = 2), and a_1 = p + q (here 5). A non-linear formula need not give an AP.
keyMoves: Substitute n=1,2,3 into the given formula
Read off a from a₁
Confirm equal differences to get d
misconceptions: Reading the constant term as a instead of computing a₁
Assuming any formula in n gives an AP

nodes.title: Apply the sum formula Sₙ = n/2[2a+(n−1)d]
nodes.brief: Find the sum of the first n terms by substituting a, n, d into Sₙ = n/2[2a + (n−1)d].
nodes.quote:
So, the sum of the first n terms of an AP is given by
S = n/2 [2a + (n – 1) d]
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — S_n = n/2 [2a + (n-1)d], used when a, d, n are known.
The sum of the first n terms is S_n = n/2 [2a + (n-1)d]. Substitute the first term a, common difference d, and number of terms n. Example: a=8, d=-5, n=22 gives S = 22/2 [16 + 21(-5)] = 11(16 - 105) = 11(-89) = -979.

PART 2 — Keep the n/2 factor and use (n-1).
Two common slips: dropping the leading n/2, and using n instead of (n-1) inside the bracket. Simplify the bracket carefully with signs (a negative d makes the bracket smaller).
keyMoves: Identify a, d, n
Substitute into n/2[2a+(n−1)d]
Simplify carefully with signs
misconceptions: Dropping the factor n/2
Using (n) instead of (n−1) inside the bracket

nodes.title: Apply the sum formula Sₙ = n/2(a+l)
nodes.brief: When the first and last terms are known, use Sₙ = n/2(a + l) to find the sum.
nodes.quote: S = n/2 (a + l)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — S_n = n/2 (a + l), using first term a and last term l.
When you know the first term a, the last term l, and the number of terms n, the sum is S_n = n/2 (a + l). This avoids needing the common difference d. Example: sum of the first 1000 positive integers = 1000/2 (1 + 1000) = 500 * 1001 = 500500.

PART 2 — Use this form only when the last term is known.
This is the right formula when a and l are given but d is not. Do not confuse l (the last term) with d (the common difference). If the last term is unknown, use S_n = n/2[2a + (n-1)d] instead.
keyMoves: Identify a (first), l (last), and n
Substitute into n/2(a+l)
misconceptions: Using this form when the last term is unknown
Confusing l with d

nodes.title: Find n given a target sum
nodes.brief: Set Sₙ = n/2[2a+(n−1)d] equal to the target sum and solve the resulting quadratic for n.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Set S_n equal to the target sum and rearrange to a quadratic in n.
When the sum is given, set n/2[2a + (n-1)d] equal to the target and simplify. Because of the (n-1) and the n/2, this becomes a QUADRATIC equation in n. Example: 78 = n/2[48 + (n-1)(-3)] simplifies to n^2 - 17n + 52 = 0.

PART 2 — Factor and keep positive-integer roots (both may be valid).
Factor the quadratic: (n-4)(n-13) = 0, so n = 4 or n = 13. Both are positive integers, so BOTH are admissible answers here. Discard only negative or fractional roots; do not throw away a valid second root.
keyMoves: Set the sum formula equal to the target
Rearrange to a quadratic in n
Factor and keep positive-integer solutions
misconceptions: Discarding a valid second root
Accepting a negative or fractional n

nodes.title: Find a missing quantity from a sum condition
nodes.brief: Use a known Sₙ together with the sum formula to solve for the missing a, d, or term.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Plug the known sum into the formula and solve for the unknown.
Given a known S_n along with some of a, d, n, substitute into S_n = n/2[2a + (n-1)d] and solve the resulting equation for the missing quantity (often d). Example: S_14 = 1050, a = 10: 1050 = 14/2[20 + 13d] = 140 + 91d, so 910 = 91d, d = 10.

PART 2 — Use the result to find a requested term.
Knowing any three of S, a, d, n lets you find the fourth; then use the nth-term formula for any term. Example: with a=10, d=10, a_20 = 10 + 19(10) = 200. Do not stop at d if a further term is asked.
keyMoves: Plug known values into the sum formula
Solve the linear equation for the unknown (often d)
Use the result in the nth-term formula if a further term is needed
misconceptions: Mis-substituting n into n/2
Stopping at d without computing the requested term

nodes.title: Solve a linear equation for one unknown
nodes.brief: Isolate the unknown by transposing and dividing to solve equations like −105 = −3n.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Collect the unknown on one side, constants on the other.
Gather all terms with the unknown on one side and all constants on the other by transposing (flipping signs across the equals sign). Example: -105 = -3n already has the unknown isolated as a term.

PART 2 — Divide by the coefficient, tracking signs.
Divide both sides by the coefficient of the unknown. Example: -105 = -3n gives n = -105/-3 = 35. Watch signs when dividing by a negative coefficient. This generic algebra is reused throughout the AP formulas.
keyMoves: Collect the unknown on one side
Divide by its coefficient
Simplify, tracking signs
misconceptions: Sign errors when dividing by a negative coefficient
Forgetting to apply an operation to both sides

refreshers.trigger: Student cannot tell which number in an equation like −105 = −3n is the coefficient of the unknown — the number to divide both sides by.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we divide: in −3n, which number is doing the multiplying to n?
ladder: When we write 3n, there is no symbol between the 3 and the n, but it still means 3 times n — multiplication is just hidden.
The number stuck in front of the letter, doing that multiplying, has a name: it is the coefficient of the unknown.
So in 3n the coefficient is 3, in 7x it is 7. And the sign travels with it: in −3n the coefficient is −3, not just 3.
If a term is written as just n on its own, there is a hidden 1 in front, so its coefficient is 1.
refreshers.answer: The coefficient of an unknown is the number multiplying it, sign included. In −3n the coefficient is −3; in 7x it is 7; and a lonely n has coefficient 1. To undo that multiplication and get the unknown by itself, you divide both sides by exactly that coefficient. That is the whole move here: in −105 = −3n the coefficient is −3, so we divide both sides by −3 to get n.
refreshers.returnCue: so the number we divide both sides by is just the coefficient sitting in front of n. back to −105 = −3n — lets divide by −3.

nodes.title: Solve a pair of simultaneous linear equations
nodes.brief: Eliminate one variable from two linear equations to find both unknowns.
nodes.quote:
Solving the pair of equations (1) and (2), we get
a = 3, d = 1
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Subtract the two equations to eliminate one variable.
When two terms of an AP give two equations in a and d, align them and SUBTRACT to cancel a (which has coefficient 1 in both). Example: a + 6d = 9 minus a + 2d = 5 gives 4d = 4, so d = 1.

PART 2 — Back-substitute for the other variable.
Put the found value back into either original equation to get the second unknown. With d = 1, a + 2(1) = 5 gives a = 3. The common errors are adding instead of subtracting, and substituting into the wrong equation.
keyMoves: Align the two equations
Subtract to eliminate one variable
Back-substitute for the other
misconceptions: Adding instead of subtracting when eliminating
Substituting the found value into the wrong equation

nodes.title: Factor a simple quadratic in n
nodes.brief: Express n²+bn+c as (n−p)(n−q)=0 to find the roots.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find a factor pair of c that sums to the middle coefficient.
For n^2 + bn + c = 0, find two integers p and q such that p*q = c and p + q = -b (the negation of b). Example: n^2 - 17n + 52 = 0 needs p*q = 52 and p + q = 17; the pair is 4 and 13.

PART 2 — Write the product of two binomials and read off the roots.
Once p and q are found, write n^2 - 17n + 52 = (n - 4)(n - 13) = 0, then set each factor to zero: n = 4 or n = 13. Keep both roots if both are positive integers; discard only negative or fractional solutions.
keyMoves: Find factor pair of the constant summing to the middle coefficient
Write as product of two binomials
Set each factor to zero for the roots
misconceptions: Sign errors in the factor pair
Missing one of the two roots

narrative:
## What I read (reviewer pass)

I re-read the full segmented chapter `jemh105.txt` (SEG 0 Introduction, SEG 1 = 5.2 Arithmetic Progressions, SEG 2 = 5.3 nth Term, SEG 3 = 5.4 Sum of First n Terms, SEG 4 = optional Exercise 5.4, SEG 5 = Summary) including all 16 worked Examples, and the shared `_maths-exams.txt` (CBSE Standard + Basic SQP and both Marking Schemes), re-locating every quoted line for the exam-sourced gates.

## Audit verdict

The draft passed almost entirely. I verified all 23 node quotes as exact substrings of their cited SEG (sec mapping: 5.2→sec 1, 5.3→sec 2, 5.4→sec 3, all correct). Specifically re-confirmed the trickier ones: `identify-first-term` (\"Since a is the first term, it can easily be written.\" — line 92, mid-paragraph substring), `list-ap-from-a-d` (the \"if the first term a is 6 …\" fragment from \"For instance if the first term a is 6 …\", lines 81–82), and `apply-sum-formula-a-l` (\"S = n/2 (a + l)\", line 393).

Exam-sourced quotes re-verified against the raw paper: Q4 7n−4 (line 42), Q19 Assertion −1,−1−1,… (line 904), Q36(iii)(A) jars S8=108 (lines 678–680 of the marking scheme). NOTE: I corrected the assertion gate's `srcLabel` from \"Basic SQP Q19\" to \"Standard SQP Q19\" — the −1,−1−1 assertion lives in the second (Standard) paper at line 904, not the Basic paper.

## Corrections applied

1. `term-from-last` gate `quote` corrected from the encoding-corrupted character to the proper en-dash: \"i.e., the 11th term from the last term is – 32.\" (line 290) — this is the canonical close of the main worked method.
2. All other quotes verified as exact verbatim substrings with ≥4 words and proper character encoding.
3. All gates verified to have consistent grader/expected/answer triples.

## Grain / coverage check

Every node is a single failable skill; no pure-exposition node survived. Re-scanned all 16 Examples for missed skills: Example 4's \"is any term 0?\" reduces to `find-which-term` (same solve-for-n), Example 16(iii) reuses `apply-sum-formula-a-d`, Example 8's reverse method is `term-from-last`. No new node warranted. The Gauss reversal derivation of Sₙ is a proof (exposition), correctly folded into the application nodes, not a student-performed gateable step.

## Edges

Single root `solve-ap-problems` (never a child). Graph is acyclic — verified all potential cycles and none found. No orphan nodes (every non-root slug appears as a child at least once), and every edge endpoint is a defined node slug. All 22 nodes are reachable from the goal by forward traversal.

## Gates

23 gates, one per node, 100% coverage. Every grader/expected/answer triple is self-consistent for the deterministic tester (numeric tol 0 or mcq with the exact correct option string). The goal gate composes model+sum (jars S8) from an answer-keyed exam case study.
absentNotes: The chapter does NOT teach these as failable skills (omitted, not failed): (1) Arithmetic mean — b = (a+c)/2 — appears only as a one-line \"A NOTE TO THE READER\" in the Summary (SEG 5), never demonstrated in a worked Example, so no node. (2) Deriving the sum formula via Gauss's reversal trick is exposition/proof in SEG 3, not a student-performed step, so it is folded into the formula-application nodes rather than a standalone \"derive the sum formula\" skill. (3) The optional Exercise 5.4 problems (first negative term, spiral semicircle lengths, terrace volume, house-number balancing) are flagged \"These exercises are not from the examination point of view\" and have no worked solutions, so no gates were sourced from them. (4) Distinguishing finite vs infinite APs is descriptive terminology only (SEG 1), not a gateable task. (5) The remark aₙ = Sₙ − Sₙ₋₁ is stated in SEG 3 but not exercised in any worked Example in this chapter (it surfaces in exam Q23(B): Sₙ = 6n − n² → d). Since no chapter Example performs it, no separate node was added; the chapter's own examples remain the spine.
